summary: Gruber argues that Apple's success with the iPhone follows Gall's Law: complex systems that work always evolve from simple systems that worked. The iPhone succeeded because Apple started with the concept of a general-purpose pocket-sized networked computer rather than starting with a phone or an iPod, creating a framework designed for future evolution. He contrasts this with Apple's failures in the 1990s, when they stopped iterating on the Mac and chased Next Big Things that never materialized. The post explains that missing features like MMS, copy/paste, and background tasking weren't failures but deliberate choices — the framework was always designed to support them when ready. Gruber is particularly confident that background multitasking will arrive once hardware catches up, noting Apple deliberately chose push notifications as a stopgap rather than shipping a subpar experience.
key_points: Gall's Law — complex working systems must evolve from simple working ones — is the formula behind Apple's decade of success
Apple's key insight with iPhone was starting with a general-purpose networked pocket computer rather than a phone or iPod, creating a framework that could support future complexity

The Newton's failure (60,000 units in all of 1996) versus the iPhone's success (30 million devices in 18 months) illustrates the difference between designing complex systems from scratch versus evolving simple ones
Apple's 1990s decline happened because they stopped iterating on the Mac and chased Next Big Things while Microsoft evolved Windows from 3.0 to 95
Background multitasking is deliberately withheld not because of software limitations but because hardware isn't ready — Apple would rather wait than ship it poorly
money_quotes: A complex system that works is invariably found to have evolved from a simple system that worked.
Nice to have is not the same thing as necessary.
It's not enough just to start simple, you have to start simple with a framework designed for future evolution and growth.
The 'phone' in 'iPhone' is much more about ubiquitous always-on wireless TCP/IP networking than it is about the 20th century conception of telephony.
Apple became too much concerned with the Next Big Thing and not enough concerned about steady evolutionary improvement to what they already had.

The way to build a complex device with all the features you want is not to start by trying to build a device with all those features, but rather to start with the fundamentals, and then iterate and evolve.
Apple could do it now, but they couldn't do it well, so they will wait.
But we ain't seen nothing yet.
key_insight: The iPhone succeeded not because of what it shipped with, but because Apple designed it as an evolvable framework — a general-purpose networked computer — rather than trying to build a feature-complete phone from scratch.
